site.title: Course Peer Observation
site.subtitle: Fundamentals of Computer Science
site.description: A collaborative peer observation program for the Fundamentals of Computer Science course, fostering teaching excellence through constructive feedback and shared learning.

home.hero_description: Enhancing teaching quality through collaborative observation, reflection, and professional development in computer science education.

items.title: Collaborative Learning
items.description: Peers observe and learn from each other's teaching methods, promoting a culture of continuous improvement.

items.title: Constructive Feedback
items.description: Structured observation forms and post-observation discussions help identify strengths and areas for growth.

items.title: Professional Development
items.description: Participants gain insights into diverse teaching strategies and pedagogical approaches in CS education.

items.title: EMI Enhancement
items.description: Support English as a Medium of Instruction (EMI) teaching practices with peer-driven quality assurance.

emi_community.title: EMI Community Plan
emi_community.subtitle: NCHU EMI Teaching Resource Center — Cross-University Bilingual Professional Development Community
emi_community.intro: This community is a Type C (Course Quality Assurance) group, composed of EMI instructors from two or more universities, dedicated to enhancing EMI teaching quality through course quality assurance mechanisms.

activities: 1 external expert (Prof. Yi Zha, Chung Yuan Christian University)
qa_items: Administer NCHU EMI Course Teaching Feedback Questionnaire with statistical and quantitative analysis
Classroom observation: observers complete the Course QA Community Observation Form
Data correlation analysis of midterm/final exam results (score distribution, Chinese/English teaching effectiveness comparison, etc.)
emi_community.rubric_description: Revised based on the EMI Course Quality Assurance Standard Rubric provided by the NCHU EMI Teaching Resource Center, following the center's rubric review process.

about.title: Peer Observation Plan
about.intro: The Course Peer Observation program for Fundamentals of Computer Science is designed to enhance teaching quality through a structured, supportive peer review process. This initiative brings together instructors to observe, reflect, and improve their teaching practices in an EMI (English as a Medium of Instruction) context.
objectives: Promote reflective teaching practices among CS educators
Provide constructive, evidence-based feedback on teaching
Share effective teaching strategies for CS foundation courses
Enhance EMI teaching skills through peer collaboration
Build a supportive community of practice in CS education

process_steps.title: Pre-Observation Meeting
process_steps.description: Observer and instructor meet to discuss the lesson plan, learning objectives, and specific areas for feedback.

process_steps.title: Classroom Observation
process_steps.description: The observer attends the class session, taking notes on teaching methods, student engagement, and learning activities.

process_steps.title: Post-Observation Discussion
process_steps.description: A reflective discussion where both parties share observations, celebrate strengths, and identify opportunities for improvement.

process_steps.title: Written Feedback
process_steps.description: The observer provides a structured written report summarizing key observations and recommendations.

principles: Confidentiality — Observations are for professional development, not evaluation
Respect — All feedback is delivered constructively and collegially
Reciprocity — Every participant serves as both observer and observed
Growth — The focus is on continuous improvement, not perfection

schedule.title: Observation Schedule
schedule.description: The following schedule outlines the planned peer observation sessions for the current semester.

form.placeholder_suggestions: What suggestions would you offer for improvement?

syllabus.title: Syllabus
syllabus.plan_name: Co-Creating a Cloud Ecosystem: Participatory Learning with AWS in Introduction to Computer Science
syllabus.overview: This course targets abstract, cognitively demanding core topics in Introduction to Computer Science (2) — operating systems, computer networks, data structures, and algorithms — using Amazon Web Services (AWS) as the teaching practice platform. Delivered in EMI mode that aligns naturally with cloud technology's English-native environment, the course combines Problem-Based Learning (PBL) with Participatory Learning strategies, transforming passive knowledge reception into co-creation of a cloud ecosystem through virtual labs on AWS.
syllabus.spiral_description: The course applies the core principle of participatory learning — 'Spiral Thinking' — guiding students through a multi-stage learning process of 'Explore → Analyze → Act → Reflect', designed in two major phases divided by the midterm exam.
syllabus.phase1_title: Phase 1: Before Midterm — Resource-Rich Guided Learning
syllabus.phase1_description: Before midterm, the course is teacher-led and resource-intensive, providing abundant learning materials to build a solid knowledge foundation. Each week cycles through four steps:

phase1_steps.label: Explore
phase1_steps.text: Instructor demonstrates how an AWS service solves a CS fundamental problem (e.g., S3 solves file storage and permissions)

phase1_steps.label: Analyze
phase1_steps.text: Students analyze architecture diagrams and English technical documentation in groups

phase1_steps.label: Act
phase1_steps.text: Hands-on AWS Console operations to deploy services

phase1_steps.label: Reflect
phase1_steps.text: Observe CloudWatch metrics, reflect on performance issues or connection failures, iterate for optimization

syllabus.phase2_title: Phase 2: After Midterm — Flipped Classroom & Cooperative Learning
syllabus.phase2_description: After midterm, the course shifts to a flipped classroom model with student-centered cooperative learning for deep exploration. Students study content independently beforehand, present their findings in class, then engage in discussion and reflection — achieving a second-round deepening of 'Spiral Thinking'.

phase2_steps.label: Pre-class Study
phase2_steps.text: Students independently study assigned resources and AWS technical documentation

phase2_steps.label: In-class Presentation
phase2_steps.text: Groups present their findings, sharing discoveries and hands-on experiences

phase2_steps.label: Discussion & Reflection
phase2_steps.text: Class-wide discussion with instructor-guided deep reflection connecting theory and practice

phase2_steps.label: Collaborative Practice
phase2_steps.text: Teams collaborate on project tasks using GitHub for version control and AWS for deployment

weeks.description: Course objectives, AWS account setup. Introduce Client-Server concept.
Launch first EC2 (Linux). SSH connection practice, Linux Command Line.
Deliberately create infinite loops, observe CPU spikes (CloudWatch), understand OS scheduling.
Analyze CIDR and IP structure. Collaboratively diagram subnet planning on whiteboard.
PBL Task 1: Build an invisible network. Implement VPC and Route Table.
Red/Blue Team exercise: Configure Security Groups. Teams swap IPs to test defenses.
Compare Array/LinkedList vs Cloud Storage. Implement S3 static web hosting.
EMI Presentation: Groups present cloud infrastructure architecture in English.
Deploy RDS. Understand B-Tree indexing impact on search performance.
Introduce AWS Lambda. Analyze event-driven vs traditional algorithm execution.
Compare local Python vs Lambda execution: time complexity and cost analysis.
Define final project (e.g., campus used-book marketplace). Draw detailed architecture.
Team development with GitHub version control and AWS deployment.
Load testing. Optimize system parameters based on test reports.
Comprehensive security review, final optimization and debugging.
English-only demo day. Industry mentors or external faculty as judges.

outcomes.text: Explain OSI model, virtualization, RESTful APIs and other core concepts in English
Independently operate AWS Console for Web Server setup, VPC configuration, with basic Linux proficiency
Shift from 'afraid to speak' to 'willing to express technical viewpoints in English' with proactive troubleshooting mindset

outputs: Cloud project portfolio: Complete cloud application system per group (architecture diagrams, source code, deployment docs)
EMI bilingual teaching guide: AWS key terminology bilingual handbook and illustrated cloud lab handouts
Demo videos: Students record English-language AWS service tutorial screencasts
